title: Tensions between the US and Europe loom large over NATO summit
gist: Ahead of the NATO summit in Ankara, transatlantic tensions are at a higher level than in any prior Trump-era summit because the Trump administration is seriously reducing US involvement in European security, is delaying defence-contractor deliveries to allies after the Iran war, and has publicly turned on previously friendly leaders (Starmer, Meloni, Merz) — pushing Europe toward rebuilding its own defence industrial base and raising doubts about the credibility of NATO's extended deterrence.

insights.claim: Tensions ahead of the 2026 NATO summit in Ankara are at a much higher level than at previous summits, including the historically tense 2018 summit when Trump first threatened to leave NATO over defence spending, because the Trump administration has now become seriously committed to reducing US involvement in European security.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Investors and policymakers tracking European defence and security risk should treat this summit as a potential inflection point rather than routine alliance friction.

insights.claim: NATO's shift to annual summits, made in response to the intensified threat level after Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, has inadvertently created a more frequent public stage for internal alliance tensions to play out.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The annual summit cadence itself is now a structural amplifier of visible alliance discord, meaning more frequent public flashpoints should be expected going forward, not just at this one meeting.
insights.locator: It is also partly a result of NATO moving to annual summits

themes.theme: Trump's break with European leaders over Iran

insights.claim: President Trump remains upset that European allies did not sufficiently support the US war in Iran, viewing this as an example of European security freeloading, since his administration believes its Middle East action benefits global security overall.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The Iran-war support gap is the proximate trigger for the current rupture and a template for how future US unilateral military action could similarly strain the alliance if allies don't fall in line.
insights.locator: President Trump remains upset that other allies

insights.claim: Trump's disappointment over the Iran war has escalated into public criticism of UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer, Italy's Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni, and Germany's Chancellor Friedrich Merz, three leaders who were previously relatively close to him and had been praised by him.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The breakdown of personal rapport with previously Trump-friendly leaders removes a channel that had been used to manage transatlantic friction, raising the odds of harder-edged US-Europe negotiations ahead.
insights.locator: President Trump has also let his disappointment

insights.claim: In response to Trump's criticism, the three targeted leaders have taken distinct tacks days before the summit: Merz has defended Germany's defence spending record, Meloni has significantly distanced herself from Trump, and Starmer has focused on building ties with other European leaders to form a counterbalance to the US.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Divergent European responses (defend, distance, coalition-build) signal fragmented rather than unified European strategy toward the US, a risk factor for alliance cohesion at the summit.
insights.locator: With only days to go until the NATO summit

themes.theme: Erosion of US reliability in European eyes

insights.claim: Confusing US threats against Greenland in January and February 2026 shocked Europeans who had not believed the US would truly turn its back on NATO, making Europe considerably more wary of Washington's intentions for the rest of the year.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The Greenland episode is identified as a specific credibility-breaking event; watch for further unilateral US territorial or security moves as a recurring driver of European de-risking from Washington.
insights.locator: The confusing US threats against Greenland

insights.claim: The US is now perceived in Europe as an inconsistent ally with a short memory, one that does not recall that Europeans invoked Article 5 solidarity to support the US after the 9/11 attacks.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: This framing of reciprocity failure is likely to be cited repeatedly by European leaders to justify reduced future deference to US preferences within NATO.
insights.quote: The US is now seen as an inconsistent ally with a short memory that does not remember Europeans’ support when the US called for Article 5 assistance after the 9/11 attacks.
insights.locator: Europe has become much more wary of Washington's intentions

insights.claim: US officials have reportedly privately warned several European allies — including the UK, Poland, Lithuania, Estonia and Germany — that their orders from American defence contractors will likely be delayed as Washington prioritizes replenishing its own stockpiles after the Iran war, deepening the sense that the US is turning away from Europe.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Concrete near-term supply-chain risk for European militaries dependent on US defence contractors, worsened by concerns that Russia could pose a NATO-level threat before European states have rebuilt sufficient capability of their own.
insights.locator: US officials have also reportedly privately warned

insights.claim: US announcements of reduced troop and equipment deployments in Europe have caused allied concern that Washington's extended deterrence guarantee to the continent is no longer as firm, even though the US is reportedly planning to affirm its ongoing Article 5 commitment at the Ankara summit.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A formal reaffirmation of Article 5 at the summit is likely to be viewed skeptically given the visible drawdown, so watch for whether allies treat the pledge as credible or largely symbolic.
insights.locator: Washington's announcements that it would be reducing troop

themes.theme: Deterrence credibility and the Cold War logic

insights.claim: It is legitimate to question whether potential adversaries will continue to view NATO's deterrence posture as credible if they can see the US visibly disengaging from the continent, since a core tenet of Cold War thinking held that the US would not risk nuclear war in Europe if it had no 'skin in the game' there — a logic that could apply equally today.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: This is a direct argument that reduced US troop presence structurally weakens deterrence credibility against Russia, independent of any formal Article 5 restatement — a key geopolitical risk variable for European security assessments.
insights.quote: a crucial tenet of Cold War thinking was that the US would not risk becoming involved in a nuclear war in Europe if it did not have any skin in the game; the same logic could also apply today
insights.locator: the extent to which potential adversaries will continue to perceive

insights.claim: The perceived US pullback is being used by European governments to justify accelerating the rebuild of indigenous European defence industrial capabilities, aiming to reduce future dependence on American arms procurement.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A structural, multi-year opportunity for European defence contractors and a corresponding headwind for US defence exporters' European order books.
insights.locator: For Europeans, this shift justifies their focus

insights.claim: European moves toward defence-industrial independence are likely to further exacerbate US-European tensions over time, because Washington has encouraged allies to spend more on defence with an implicit expectation that spending flows to American equipment purchases, and the US may grow less supportive of a more independently secure Europe if European orders decline.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A second-order, self-reinforcing tension: US calls for higher European defence spending could backfire on US defence exporters and on the broader alliance relationship if that spending is redirected to European suppliers instead of American ones.
insights.locator: this increasing independence will undoubtedly exacerbate tensions

insights.claim: European allies remain divided on how to manage the relationship with an increasingly erratic US; ahead of the 2025 NATO summit in The Hague there was reluctance about Secretary General Mark Rutte's flattery-based approach to managing Trump, though the prevailing view was that results justified the method.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Watch whether the 'flattery works' consensus holds at Ankara given the fresh Trump-Europe rifts, or whether leaders like Starmer's coalition-building approach signals a shift away from that strategy.
